name: Valle de Valverde, Río Tera, Mozar y sierra de las Cavernas — circular desde Tábara
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Tábara traverse a varied landscape characterized by river valleys, rolling hills, and sections of more significant elevation. The region features routes along the Río Tera and Aliste River, providing picturesque scenery and generally accessible riding conditions. Cyclists can also explore areas around the Sierra de la Culebra and Sierra de las Cavernas, which present more challenging ascents and expansive views. This mix of terrain offers options for different fitness levels, from routes with gentle gradients to those with substantial climbs. Best road cycling routes around Tábara The most popular road cycling route is Tábara – tour around Mózar – Manganeses de la Polvorosa – Benavente, a 64.9 miles (104.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 10 minutes to complete. This moderate route connects several towns and offers varied scenery. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal, Río Tera y Valle de Valverde — circular por la Sierra de Culebra, a moderate 55.5 miles (89.3 km) path. This route circles the Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal and traverses the Sierra de la Culebra. Local road cyclists also love the Tabara - tour around Aliste river and Fonfria, a 49.3 miles (79.3 km) trail leading through the Aliste River valley, often completed in about 3 hours 47 minutes. This difficult route features significant elevation changes. Road cycling around Tábara is defined by routes through river valleys, around the Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal, and into the Sierra de la Culebra. The network offers options for both easy rides and more challenging excursions with substantial elevation gains. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 30 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Tábara's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Tábara?
A: There are over 15 road cycling routes available around Tábara, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy rides to more challenging excursions.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Tábara?
A: Road cycling routes in Tábara feature a varied landscape. You'll find picturesque rides through river valleys like the Río Tera and Aliste River, as well as more challenging ascents in areas such as the Sierra de la Culebra and Sierra de las Cavernas. The terrain includes both flat sections and significant elevation gains, providing a dynamic experience.

Q: Are there any easy road cycling routes suitable for beginners in Tábara?
A: Yes, Tábara offers easy road cycling routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a more relaxed ride. For instance, the route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368925090/from-ferreras-de-arriba-to-ferreras-de-abajo-circular-from-villardeciervos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;From Ferreras de Arriba to Ferreras de Abajo — circular from Villardeciervos&lt;/a&gt; is classified as easy, covering about 42.5 km with moderate elevation changes.

Q: What are some challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: For experienced riders looking for a challenge, Tábara has routes with significant elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1025807411/tabara-tour-around-aliste-river-and-fonfria" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tabara - tour around Aliste river and Fonfria&lt;/a&gt; route, for example, is classified as difficult, featuring over 1000 meters of ascent over its 79.3 km length.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes around Tábara?
A: Yes, Tábara offers several circular road cycling routes. A notable option is &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368936099/embalse-de-nuestra-senora-de-agavanzal-rio-tera-y-valle-de-valverde-circular-por-la-sierra-de-culebra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal, Río Tera y Valle de Valverde — circular por la Sierra de Culebra&lt;/a&gt;, which circles the Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal and traverses the Sierra de la Culebra, offering diverse scenery.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see while cycling in Tábara?
A: While cycling in Tábara, you can enjoy scenic views along the Río Tera and Aliste River valleys. The region also features the Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal and the mountainous landscapes of the Sierra de la Culebra and Sierra de las Cavernas. Additionally, you might encounter historical sites such as the Romanesque church Santa María de Tábara in the town itself. Specific highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/769731" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quintos Bridge Over the Esla River&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3854552" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Esla River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other road cyclists say about Tábara?
A: The road cycling routes around Tábara are well-regarded by the komoot community, with over 30 road cyclists having explored the area. Users often praise the varied terrain, from river valleys to challenging mountain sections, and the picturesque scenery that makes for an engaging ride.

Q: Can I find routes that connect different towns or villages?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Tábara connect various towns and villages, allowing you to experience the local culture and scenery. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1024549312/tabara-tour-around-mozar-manganeses-de-la-polvorosa-benavente" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tábara – tour around Mózar – Manganeses de la Polvorosa – Benavente&lt;/a&gt; route, which offers a journey through several communities with varied landscapes.

Q: Are there routes that explore the Valle de Valverde?
A: Yes, the Valle de Valverde is a prominent feature in some of Tábara's road cycling routes. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368940212/valle-de-valverde-rio-tera-mozar-y-sierra-de-las-cavernas-circular-desde-tabara" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valle de Valverde, Río Tera, Mozar y sierra de las Cavernas — circular desde Tábara&lt;/a&gt; specifically takes you through this scenic valley, alongside the Río Tera, and into the Sierra de las Cavernas.

Q: What is the typical duration for road cycling routes in Tábara?
A: The duration of road cycling routes in Tábara varies significantly based on distance and difficulty. Easy routes might take around 1.5 to 2 hours, while moderate routes can range from 3 to 4 hours. More difficult and longer routes, such as the 79.3 km Aliste River tour, can take nearly 4 hours to complete.

Q: Are there any routes that offer views of reservoirs?
A: Yes, you can find routes that provide scenic views of local reservoirs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1368936099/embalse-de-nuestra-senora-de-agavanzal-rio-tera-y-valle-de-valverde-circular-por-la-sierra-de-culebra" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal, Río Tera y Valle de Valverde — circular por la Sierra de Culebra&lt;/a&gt; route is a great option, as it circles the Embalse de Nuestra Señora de Agavanzal, offering beautiful waterside vistas.
tips: The church of Santa Marta de Tera is located on the Jacobean route Camino Sanabrés to Santiago, a branch of the Vía de la Plata. During the spring and autumn equinoxes, the chapel is illuminated by sunlight that enters through the oculus of the main pediment, an event that attracts many tourists.
Bridge over the Tera river between Sitrama and Santibáñez de Tera. The river is a tributary of the Esla river, a natural park of special conservation.

Mózar is a small town located at the confluence of the Tera and Órbigo rivers in the Benavente valley. Famous for its natural activities, there are several campsites near the river beaches for bathing and abundant places for trout fishing.

Tábara, located in the northwest of the province of Zamora (Castilla y León), is a town with a rich medieval history, a stunning natural setting, and a significant cultural heritage.
Origins: These date back to the Bronze Age and Roman period.
Middle Ages: It was an important monastic center with the Monastery of San Salvador de Tábara, founded by Saint Froilán in the 9th century at the behest of Alfonso III.
